title: Hirschsprung Disease: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Hirschsprung Disease
Identify key diagnostic features of Hirschsprung Disease
Apply clinical reasoning to Hirschsprung Disease management
Recognize common pitfalls in Hirschsprung Disease

sections.title: Overview of Hirschsprung Disease
sections.content:
**Hirschsprung Disease**

Neural crest cell migration failure, aganglionic bowel, transition zone, RET gene, suction rectal biopsy

**Key Points:**
• Failed migration of neural crest cells → absent ganglion cells in Meissner and Auerbach plexuses
• Always involves rectum, extends proximally (short-segment most common)
• Functional obstruction: aganglionic segment stays contracted

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Failed migration of neural crest cells → absent ganglion cells in Meissner and Auerbach plexuses
• Always involves rectum, extends proximally (short-segment most common)

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Functional obstruction: aganglionic segment stays contracted
• Diagnosis: suction rectal biopsy (absent ganglion cells, hypertrophied nerve fibers)
• RET proto-oncogene mutations; Associated with Down syndrome (trisomy 21)

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Hirschsprung Disease:**

• Functional obstruction: aganglionic segment stays contracted
• Diagnosis: suction rectal biopsy (absent ganglion cells, hypertrophied nerve fibers)
• RET proto-oncogene mutations; Associated with Down syndrome (trisomy 21)

mcqs.question: Which of the following is MOST characteristic of Hirschsprung Disease?

options.text: Failed migration of neural crest cells → absent ganglion cells in Meissner and Auerbach plexuses
options.explanation: Failed migration of neural crest cells → absent ganglion cells in Meissner and Auerbach plexuses

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

mcqs.question: What is the KEY diagnostic or management consideration for Hirschsprung Disease?

options.text: RET proto-oncogene mutations; Associated with Down syndrome (trisomy 21)
options.explanation: RET proto-oncogene mutations; Associated with Down syndrome (trisomy 21)

options.text: Immediate surgical intervention in all cases

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with Hirschsprung Disease. Which finding would you expect?

options.text: Always involves rectum, extends proximally (short-segment most common)
options.explanation: Always involves rectum, extends proximally (short-segment most common)
